policy_statement: PSC must issue a comprehensive review of the renewable energy program by July 1, 2024 and every two years thereafter, covering progress toward 70% renewable electricity by 2030 and 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040 and including program progress for 9 GW offshore wind (2035), 6 GW solar PV (2025), and 3 GW energy storage (2030).
mechanism_description: The PSC will issue recurring reviews that track progress toward renewable and zero-emission electricity goals and related procurement programs, supporting accountability and course-correction for grid decarbonization.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance oversight & accountability → faster/stronger renewable & zero-emission electricity deployment → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in activity/system: The regulator (e.g., PSC) institutionalizes recurring reviews, reporting, and evaluation of renewable/zero-emission electricity targets and procurement programs, creating transparency and accountability for implementers and market actors. (2) Mediating process: Better monitoring reveals gaps, delays, and underperformance; this supports corrective actions (rule updates, procurement adjustments, set, budget/program redesign) that increase the pace and reliability of clean capacity additions and grid transition. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Higher share of renewable/zero-emission electricity and reduced reliance on fossil generation, leading to lower CO₂e emissions from the electricity sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is accelerating electricity grid decarbonization, thereby reducing power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Oversight/reporting → improved accountability and course-correction → more/earlier renewable and zero-emission electricity deployment → reduced fossil generation → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it is a governance process and can improve “efficiency” in program management, the main climate outcome is emissions reduction via grid decarbonization, which is Mitigation (not Resource Efficiency, Adaptation, or Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If reviews are purely informational with no influence on decisions, climate impact could be indirect/weak; however, the mechanism as stated explicitly supports accountability and course-correction toward decarbonization targets, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any renewable/zero-emission electricity program review/progress-tracking requirement is designed to drive decarbonization progress and thus primarily targets emissions reductions from electricity supply.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific mitigation subtypes (e.g., renewable buildout, procurement acceleration, compliance/enforcement) depend on policy-level evidence about how findings trigger actions, which is not assigned at this mechanism-only stage.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be borderline “enabling/administrative” if the review has no enforcement or decision hooks; some instances may mainly improve transparency rather than directly change generation mix. Still, the dominant intended pathway across this mechanism is enabling faster grid decarbonization (mitigation).
key_indicators: "issue a comprehensive review"; "every two years thereafter"; "including progress in meeting the overall targets for 70% renewable electricity by 2030 and 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040"; "progress update"; "programs the PSC has established to require procurement of 9 GW of offshore wind... 6 GW of solar PV... 3 GW of energy storage"
instance_edge_case_notes: The text references procurement requirements, but the operative requirement here is the biennial review/progress reporting; classification focuses on the reporting/oversight instrument rather than the underlying procurement mandates. If those procurement requirements were the policy statement, instrument_type could shift to procurement-requirement/regulatory-mandate.
